directions.name: Reopen The Auxiliary 256x128 Hot-Band Schedule On The Restored Best Base
directions.hypothesis: The latest measured specialization run 20260420_183102_bf16_gemm_v1_29a10ec closes the current PTX inner-loop family on the restored grouped-rows-4 lineage: hot-band registers climbed from 200 on the accepted 2e4dd24 base to 242, long-scoreboard stalls jumped from 7.43% to 15.07%, barrier stalls rose from 5.61% to 6.75%, and end-to-end runtime regressed to 25.6650238 ms. The best next move is therefore to accept a materially different hot-band family rather than another PTX-loop tweak. The auxiliary 256x128 branch is the strongest alternate family in measured history, with a 24.69619179 ms result on 20260420_112149_bf16_gemm_v1_9a4bb85, so retuning that schedule on top of the restored 2e4dd24 base offers the best remaining upside.
directions.expected_bottleneck: The main risk is that the wider 256x128 CTA can trade away the grouped-row locality win unless its K-loop cadence and export timing are retuned for the current base. If it fails, the signature will be barrier or scoreboard pressure moving from the current PTX hot band into the auxiliary schedule rather than a clean runtime gain.
directions.risk: Medium. This is a real family pivot, but it has positive measured ancestry and avoids spending another round on the now-regressed fixed-452-tile PTX specialization path. The main failure mode is repeating the slower naive auxiliary-default restore that later measured 31.86790371 ms, so this should be treated as a schedule retune, not a blind path swap.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the retuned 256x128 branch still lands above the accepted 24.44441605 ms base or if it cannot keep hot-band register pressure and long-scoreboard stalls below the current 29a10ec regression signature after one bounded retune pass.

directions.name: Use The Non-PTX 128x128 Sibling As A Clean Control Path
directions.hypothesis: The regressed 29a10ec run suggests the current PTX microkernel path is now scheduler-fragile on this lineage, not just slightly mistuned. The adjacent non-PTX 128x128 sibling keeps the same broad tile family while removing the PTX-microkernel accumulate and export helpers that the last three rounds stressed. That makes it the cleanest control experiment for separating grouped-row launch benefits from PTX-specific register and scoreboard inflation.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Because the sibling kernel currently uses plain block ordering instead of the grouped-row physical-to-logical remap, it may give back the locality benefit that made 2e4dd24 fast even if it lowers registers. The expected bottleneck is therefore a trade between reduced scoreboard pressure and weaker L2 / launch-order reuse.
directions.risk: Medium-high. Historical evidence for this family is weaker than dir_01: the prior control-path attempt 20260420_162632_bf16_gemm_v1_948fc5c only reached 26.70438385 ms. It is still worth keeping as the second-ranked option because it directly tests whether PTX-path complexity is the dominant source of the new regression.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the sibling control path remains materially slower than the 2e4dd24 base without a clear register or scoreboard reduction, because that would confirm the grouped PTX branch is still the better underlying family.

directions.name: Retune The Hot-Band/Peeled Seam Without Touching The PTX Loop
directions.hypothesis: Rounds 3 through 5 show that touching the PTX hot-band loop itself has been consistently negative on the restored grouped-rows-4 base. A lower-risk fallback is to leave that hot loop alone and only move the hot-band versus peeled-row seam, using the existing peeled launcher and dispatch math to test whether the last 64 hot rows are amplifying the barrier and scoreboard tax seen in 29a10ec.
directions.expected_bottleneck: This branch is bounded by the peeled kernel becoming the new bottleneck. If the seam moves too far upward, the 384-wide peeled path can easily erase any hot-band relief through extra barrier time and worse combined kernel balance.
directions.risk: Medium. This is safer than reopening the broad fixed-main 64x384 override, but it is still only a deferred fallback because prior seam experiments on this lineage plateaued around the mid-25.6 ms range, well above the current 24.44441605 ms best.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if enlarging the peeled band increases combined hot-band plus peeled time or leaves the peeled kernel with higher barrier share and no offsetting runtime win.

notes: Round 6/50 diagnosis for 20260420_183102_bf16_gemm_v1_29a10ec. Human-review audit: state/human_review.md currently contains workflow and approval guidance but no concrete user-supplied idea-family bullets to evaluate one by one, so there are no explicit human ideas to accept, defer, or reject this round; ranking is therefore driven by measured evidence. The latest run explicitly rejects more fixed-452-tile PTX hot-loop surgery on this lineage: versus the accepted 20260420_180146_bf16_gemm_v1_2e4dd24 base, the hot-band kernel moved from 200 to 242 registers per thread, long-scoreboard stalls rose from 7.43% to 15.07%, barrier stalls rose from 5.61% to 6.75%, tensor active slipped from 48.16% to 47.90%, and end-to-end runtime regressed from 24.44441605 ms to 25.6650238 ms. Accepted for this round: pivot to alternate hot-band execution families, with the auxiliary 256x128 schedule as the primary branch because it has the strongest positive measured ancestry outside the current PTX microkernel family. Deferred: seam-only launch-split retunes that preserve the existing PTX loop but probably cap out below the accepted best. Rejected for this round: additional PTX-loop handoff, export-live-range, or fixed-shape specialization variants on the restored grouped-rows-4 base.
